input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A generic building serving as the cyber‑physical asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain; the physical twin represents the building and its lifecycle from design through operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of sensor data, interactive 3‑D simulation, life‑cycle‑wide optimization of building operations, predictive analytics for fault detection and performance forecasting, decision‑support dashboards for facility managers, and extensible custom services via a plug‑in architecture.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation integrates Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto for MQTT‑based real‑time data exchange and twin state management, employs Unreal Engine for high‑fidelity interactive visualization, utilizes Building Information Modeling (BIM) as the static asset model source, and applies Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) techniques to generate adapters and service code.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT is designed to operate on a real‑time scale: physical sensor streams are ingested continuously and fed to the digital replica with low latency, enabling live synchronization and immediate analytical feedback. The architecture requires that data be synchronized as soon as it becomes available, supporting real‑time insights and predictive analytics; no explicit synchronization interval is prescribed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a model‑driven orchestration pattern centred on a central integration hub. Static BIM models are combined with runtime IoT data through bidirectional data exchange facilitated by Eclipse Hono (for protocol translation and connectivity) and Eclipse Ditto (for digital twin state management). The hub orchestrates services that expose standardized REST/WS APIs, enabling the Unreal Engine visualisation component to render the virtual environment. Resource management, component integration and system‑wide coordination are handled within this modular hub, which links the data acquisition, state management and visualisation layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework uses enriched BIM models as the source of both geometric and behavioral representations. Building Control Engineers embed semantic descriptions of sensors, actuators and controllers directly into the BIM file, producing a unified model that combines static geometry with device‑level operational metadata. Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) transformations extract these enriched BIM artefacts and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts, including runtime models (e.g., control logic scripts) and data schemas. Real‑time data exchange is supported, providing continuous synchronization of sensor readings and actuator states between the physical building control system (BCS) and its digital counterpart. The resulting DT therefore consists of geometric models (building layout, asset locations), analytical/behavioral models (control algorithms, occupancy forecasts), and managed data streams originating from post‑occupancy sensor feeds.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators and controllers are first modelled as device entities inside the enriched BIM model by the Building Control Engineer. Each actuator entry includes its type (actuator or controller) and the set of commandable actions that can be triggered remotely. At runtime the Facility Manager can invoke these actions through the Digital Twin’s interactive interface, which forwards the command over the framework’s standardized extension points to the corresponding physical endpoint. The description does not enumerate specific hardware, actuation ranges, or safety constraints; the twin abstracts the actuation capability as a remotely executable command linked to the BIM‑defined device.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are likewise introduced in the BIM model as semantic objects that describe the measured physical quantity. The framework establishes a real‑time data‑exchange channel that continuously streams the sensed values from the physical devices to the Digital Twin, enabling up‑to‑date state representation. Spatial distribution, sampling frequency, accuracy and transmission rates are not explicitly detailed in the document; the twin relies on the generic real‑time synchronization mechanism to collect sensor data as defined by the BIM‑based sensor model.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained by binding the enriched BIM semantic model to live sensor/actuator streams through the MDE‑driven transformation pipeline; any deviation between the physical state and its digital representation is detected by the continuous real‑time synchronization layer. Validation is performed implicitly when the generated DT artifacts are deployed: the framework checks that each BIM‑defined device has a corresponding runtime endpoint and that control logic can be instantiated without manual intervention. Uncertainty sources such as stale sensor readings are mitigated by the ongoing synchronization, while semantic consistency between the BIM model and the twin is the primary quality‑assurance criterion described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronises sensor and actuator readings from the physical building with the digital twin, ensuring the virtual model reflects the current state of the building. The exchange is described as continuous and real‑time, but the document does not specify the exact data types transmitted, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event‑trigger conditions, data‑quality assurance methods, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional data exchange allows the digital twin to send control commands back to the physical building for monitoring and actuation, enabling Facility Managers to perform real‑time control. The document mentions this capability but does not provide details on command types, message formats, validation or authentication mechanisms, feedback loops, conflict‑resolution strategies, or confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system is built on a modular architecture with standardized extension points that support interoperability across heterogeneous systems and scalability from component to infrastructure level. Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability. However, the document does not detail the underlying communication protocols, network topology, bandwidth or latency requirements, reliability or redundancy mechanisms, nor the security protocols used for network communication.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a model‑driven lifecycle that starts with device modeling in the BIM environment by the Building Control Engineer (definition of sensors, actuators, controllers). Enriched BIM models are then processed by model‑driven engineering (MDE) tooling to extract a structured semantic representation. The MDE step automatically generates deployable Digital Twin artifacts (ontology instances, service descriptors) which are instantiated in the runtime environment. During operation the Twin is kept in continuous synchronization with the physical building via real‑time data exchange. The Twin provides interactive visualization for monitoring and control. Extensions can be added through standardized plug‑in points, enabling evolution of the Twin throughout its operational phase. Governance is realized through role‑based responsibilities (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) and the modular architecture that isolates lifecycle stages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Development follows a model‑driven engineering (MDE) methodology. Building Control Engineers model devices and control logic directly in BIM; the model‑driven deployment framework extracts the enriched BIM into formal representations and automatically generates the Digital Twin code base. DT Engineers create custom services and plug‑ins that are integrated through the framework’s standardized extension points. The architecture is modular, allowing runtime addition or replacement of services, thereby supporting evolutionary updates. The document does not detail explicit quality‑assurance, testing, or version‑control mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Twin supplies decision support through continuous real‑time data exchange that synchronizes sensor readings and actuator states, and through immersive interactive visualizations that allow Facility Managers to monitor building performance and issue control commands. The framework’s analytics extensions can be plugged in to generate higher‑level insights, although specific analytics methods are not enumerated. Recommendations are presented through the visualization interface; confidence measures are not described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach is built on Building Information Modeling (BIM), which the document cites as the emerging industry standard for construction data exchange. The framework leverages BIM as the foundational representation, enriches it with control‑logic semantics, and uses standardized extension points to ensure interoperability of custom services. No additional external standards (e.g., OPC UA, ISO 19650) are mentioned in the provided text.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the domain (AECO) and the nature of the physical asset (a building) including its lifecycle from design to refurbishment. While the text mentions a 'Living Lab case study' as a specific instance, the description accurately reflects the general system type described. Supported by quotes: 'virtual representations of physical assets', 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments'.

The extraction is largely accurate, covering real-time monitoring, simulation, life-cycle optimization, predictive analytics, and extensible services, all of which are mentioned in the text. However, it adds unsupported details such as 'fault detection' and 'performance forecasting', which are not explicitly found in the source snippets. 'Decision-support dashboards' are inferred from 'enhanced decision-making capabilities' and 'visualization' rather than explicitly stated.

The extraction correctly lists the key tools: Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, Unreal Engine, BIM, and MDE. It accurately describes their roles (real-time data exchange, visualization, model source). However, it specifies 'MQTT-based' communication, which is not mentioned in the text, and describes the generation of 'adapters and service code' whereas the text refers more generally to 'runtime interfaces' and 'runtime artifacts'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic correctly identifies the real-time nature of the Digital Twin, citing continuous synchronization and low latency for live feedback. This is supported by the text mentioning 'real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', and 'continuous synchronization with physical assets through sensor data'. The deduction that no specific interval is prescribed is reasonable given the emphasis on continuous/real-time operation without explicit time intervals in the text.

The document describes a framework for deploying DTs and creating 'a virtual representation of a physical asset'. It does not provide information regarding multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or decentralized architectures. Thus, 'Not in Document' is correct.

The description accurately captures the model-driven approach, the central integration hub, and the specific components involved (BIM, Eclipse Hono/Ditto, Unreal Engine). Evidence includes 'DT Framework acts as a central hub' and 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'. However, the specific mention of 'REST/WS APIs' is not supported by the provided text, which instead refers to 'standardized extension points' and 'runtime interfaces'. This prevents a perfect score.

The document focuses on the framework's internal architecture and its vertical connection to physical assets via 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for IoT connectivity. While it mentions goals like 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems', it does not describe specific integration patterns, protocols, or dependencies with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins (horizontal integration). The description focuses on the integration of BIM, middleware, and visualization rather than cross-system horizontal links.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

Quotes: 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The description correctly identifies the roles involved (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager), the modeling of actuators within the BIM environment, and the use of standardized extension points for command invocation. It accurately notes that the text abstracts the hardware details. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... Models and configures devices, defines the control logic', 'Facility Manager... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extraction aligns well with the source, noting that sensors are semantic objects in the BIM model and that real-time data exchange is used. It correctly points out the absence of specific technical specifications like sampling rates in the provided text. Supported by quotes: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

While the extraction correctly identifies the general mechanism of fidelity maintenance through real-time synchronization of BIM models, it includes specific unsupported details regarding validation and uncertainty. The text mentions validating the model before configuration but does not describe a runtime check for endpoints or mention 'stale sensor readings' as a specific mitigated uncertainty. Supported by quotes: 'synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'. Unsupported claims: 'framework checks that each BIM-defined device has a corresponding runtime endpoint', 'Uncertainty sources such as stale sensor readings'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

It accurately assesses that the document omits specific network infrastructure details like topology, bandwidth, and security protocols, although it mentions the integration of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for connectivity. Supported by quotes: 'modular architecture', 'standardized extension points', 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. Therefore, this assessment is correct.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
